title: Headlight Restoration Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Headlight Restoration Business — Real Costs & Earnings
metaDescription: Honest guide to starting a headlight restoration business: real startup costs, realistic earnings, how to find customers, and why durability is the biggest pitfall.
bestFor: People who want an extremely low-cost mobile side gig and like quick, visible before/after results
biggestRisk: Cheap restorations that yellow again within months, killing referrals and reviews
whatItIs: Headlight restoration is the process of removing the cloudy, yellowed oxidation that builds up on polycarbonate headlight lenses, then sanding, polishing, and sealing them so they look clear and pass inspection again. Foggy headlights are an extremely common problem on vehicles five years and older, and replacement lenses or assemblies often cost hundreds of dollars, so a $60 to $150 restoration is an easy sell. It is one of the cheapest automotive businesses to start because the work is done by hand or with a basic drill and a small kit of sandpaper, compound, and a UV-cured clear coat. Most people run it as a mobile add-on alongside car detailing or as a standalone weekend service, not as a sole full-time income.
dailyReality: A single restoration takes 20 to 45 minutes per pair once you are practiced. You wet-sand the lens through progressively finer grits, polish with compound, then apply a sealant or sprayable UV clear coat and let it cure. Because each job is small, your day is mostly driving between customers, doing fast turnarounds in driveways and parking lots, and collecting payment on the spot. You are bent over a bumper, often in the sun, dealing with masking tape and dust. The selling is constant: a lot of your time goes to posting before/after photos, answering messages, and bundling restoration into bigger detailing or used-car-lot jobs.

startupCostsItemized.item: UV-cured clear coat or quality sealant kit
Masking tape, microfiber towels, spray bottles, gloves
Google Business Profile and basic flyers

realEarnings.yearOne: Run part-time, most beginners earn $600 to $2,000 per month. At $60 to $120 per car and a few cars per week from local ads and word of mouth, this is realistically a side income, not a salary, in year one.
realEarnings.experienced: Operators who pair it with detailing, build a route, or land used-car-lot accounts report $2,500 to $5,000 per month. The volume comes from bundling, repeat fleet work, and a steady review-fed pipeline rather than higher per-car prices.
realEarnings.topEarners: The strongest standalone restoration specialists who service dealerships and fleets, or who fold it into a busy detailing brand, can clear $6,000 to $10,000+ per month, but at that point it is usually one service line inside a larger automotive business with a helper, not a one-person headlight-only operation.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Volume and bundling matter far more than per-job price. Whether you use a durable UV clear coat (lasts years) versus a cheap polish that fogs again in months decides your reviews, referrals, and repeat-account survival.
realEarnings.perHour: On the actual work, $60 to $150 per hour is common because jobs are quick. Counting driving, quoting, and marketing, realistic blended pay is closer to $30 to $70 per hour for a solo mobile operator.

howToStart: Week 1: Buy a basic sanding/polishing kit and a UV clear coat. Practice on a junkyard lens or your own car and a few friends' cars until your results are clear, even, and hold up. Get general liability insurance before charging anyone.

Week 2: Take sharp before/after photos and set up a Google Business Profile and a Facebook/Nextdoor presence. Price simply ($60 to $120 per pair) and offer your first 10 customers a small launch discount in exchange for photos and reviews.
Month 1: Knock out 10 to 20 paid jobs and ask for a review the moment each customer sees the result. Track how long jobs actually take so you stop underpricing the slow, badly oxidized ones.

Days 30 to 90: Pitch local detailers, independent used-car lots, and small fleets on a per-vehicle rate, and add the service to any detailing or mobile-wash work you already do. Decide whether to upgrade to a faster machine setup based on real volume.

mustHave: Patience and a steady hand for sanding evenly without burning through the lens coating
Comfort approaching strangers and selling a small, fast service
Reliability for quick mobile turnarounds on the customer's schedule
canLearn: Correct grit progression and polishing technique (a weekend of practice)
How to apply UV-cured clear coat without runs or fisheyes
Reading which lenses are restorable versus too cracked or internally fogged to fix
separators: Using durable sealing methods that last years, so you build referrals instead of callbacks
Bundling and upselling into detailing, trim restoration, or fleet accounts for volume
Knowing when to say no to a lens that will not hold, protecting your reputation

whatPeopleGetWrong: Polishing only and skipping a real UV-protective coating, so the lenses re-yellow within months and the phone stops ringing
Treating headlight restoration as a full-time income on its own when the per-job ceiling is too low without bundling or volume
Burning through the lens's factory hard coat or leaving sanding swirls by rushing the grit progression
Not setting customer expectations on heavily cracked or internally fogged lenses that cannot be saved by restoration
Underpricing badly oxidized jobs that take twice as long as a light refresh
Skipping insurance, then having no protection if a slipped sander or overspray damages paint or trim

toolsAndEquipment.note: The core consumable. Buy a full grit range so you can match the level of oxidation.
Speeds the polish step dramatically versus hand work; optional at first.
Brings clarity back before sealing. A small amount goes a long way.
The single most important durability decision. Cheaper sealants fog again fast.
Protect surrounding paint and keep work clean. Cheap and essential.
A small inverter or battery tools let you work anywhere. Add when you go fully mobile.

methods: A Google Business Profile and Facebook/Nextdoor posts with dramatic before/after photos, which sell this service better than any other channel
Partnering with local detailers and mobile car washes who want to offer it without doing it themselves
Independent used-car lots and small fleets that need cheap, fast curb-appeal fixes per vehicle
Parking-lot and apartment-complex flyering, since foggy headlights are visible on parked cars
Asking every customer for a review on the spot, since the visible result makes people happy to oblige
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Owners of vehicles roughly five-plus years old, used-car lots prepping inventory, and anyone failing or worried about a state inspection. Suburban neighborhoods and budget used-car dealers are the densest sources.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: First paid jobs usually come within days of posting good photos locally. A steady, referral-fed and account-fed pipeline typically takes two to four months of consistent work.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Paid broad social ads, a logo or polished website before you have any reviews, and trying to compete on price with the $20 cheap-polish crowd. Photos, reviews, and durable results convert; branding does not.

howItScales.toFullTime: Possible but limited as a standalone service because per-job price is low. Most people who go full-time fold restoration into a broader detailing or mobile-wash business, or chase volume through dealer and fleet accounts.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Hard to step back from a headlight-only model, but the work is easy to teach, so a helper can double your throughput. True hands-off operation generally requires building it into a larger detailing brand with multiple service lines.
howItScales.sellable: On its own, a headlight-only operation is barely sellable because it is essentially you and a kit. As part of a detailing business with recurring accounts and a brand, it adds value and can be sold with that larger company.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Adding higher-ticket services (detailing, trim and plastic restoration, ceramic coating), locking in repeat dealer and fleet accounts, and systemizing the work so a trained helper produces consistent, durable results.

strongFit: You want the cheapest possible entry into automotive services and like quick, visible wins
You already detail cars or want a simple add-on to an existing mobile service
You are comfortable selling small jobs and asking for reviews
You are patient enough to do the sanding right rather than rushing
poorFit: You need this to replace a full-time salary by itself
You dislike repetitive, detail-oriented hand work
You are not willing to invest in a coating that actually lasts
You want a hands-off or passive income source
askYourself: Am I willing to do enough volume or bundling to make low per-job prices add up?
Will I use durable sealing methods even though they cost a bit more per car?
Is there a steady supply of older cars, used-car lots, or fleets near me to serve?

faqs.question: How long does a headlight restoration actually last?
faqs.answer: It depends entirely on the sealing step. A simple polish with no protection often re-yellows in two to six months. A properly applied UV-cured clear coat can last two to five years. The coating you choose is the difference between repeat referrals and constant complaints.

faqs.question: Can I really start this for under $200?
faqs.answer: Yes. A sandpaper assortment, compound, applicators, and a UV clear coat kit can be bought for well under $200, and you may already own a drill. Insurance is the main meaningful add-on. The low entry cost is exactly why bundling and volume matter so much for actual income.

faqs.question: Is headlight restoration a good full-time business?
faqs.answer: On its own, rarely. The per-job price is low, so a full-time income requires high volume, fleet/dealer accounts, or bundling with detailing. Most successful operators treat it as a fast, low-cost side service or one line inside a larger automotive business.

faqs.question: What should I charge per car?
faqs.answer: Common pricing is $60 to $120 per pair for residential customers, with discounted per-vehicle rates for dealers and fleets doing volume. Time your real jobs so heavily oxidized lenses do not turn into money-losers, and charge more for severe cases.

faqs.question: Can every foggy headlight be fixed?
faqs.answer: No. Restoration handles surface oxidation, but lenses that are cracked, have internal fogging or moisture, or have a failed coating you cannot remove may need replacement. Being honest about what you can and cannot fix protects your reputation.

faqs.question: Do I need a license to do this?
faqs.answer: Most areas require only a general business registration and liability insurance, not a specialized automotive license, for cosmetic exterior work like this. Always confirm local rules, especially if you plan to work with dealers or operate on commercial lots.

faqs.question: Should I offer it mobile or have customers come to me?
faqs.answer: Mobile is usually the better fit because the job is quick and customers love the convenience of having it done in their driveway or office lot. You only need a small kit and, for full flexibility, portable power. Many operators do both.
